sentences.text: Hey, Kelly.
Hey, Matt.
How are you?
Pretty good.
Good.
You look… you look like you're busy there, in the background.
We just have this big screen we project on all day.
Awesome, awesome.
Is that, like, the assets and how they're performing, or what do you have back there?
That currently… yep, that's what we're showing.
Awesome, awesome.
Great to be introduced.
Yeah, you too.

We've, … spent a fair amount of time with, ApartmentIQ so far, so, hopefully this should be, pretty quick, but yeah, I'm on the, data science team, at Pennybacker, and so… we, like, from my point of view, we're just trying to get access to the API through Apartments IQ, through our kind of existing, account with IPS.

And, … yeah, really just for data science projects and machine learning purposes, just would love to get as much property-level data as we can out of the API, and so… Just would like to talk through options there and get a quote for that.
Yeah, and how are you all thinking about this?
So, there's, I guess, several solutions, but, first thing I wanted to understand is just, like, the scope.
Like, if you're thinking.
i.
e, like, full MSA data, or zip code data, or the competitive properties, that have been set up by IPS around the subject properties, that's another data option as well.
So, maybe that's a good starting point.
Yeah, and that's… and that's great that all of those are available.
Yeah, I think we would… like… both property-level data and also the ability to roll up to zip codes and MSAs.
Okay.
And then any, like, any filter sets, because there's a few different, like, options for us to get this to you.
One of them would be, like, full-blown API.
The other and most common for most customers of IQ is what we call, like, a, … daily data sync, and that is just like a… this is Moose, by the way.
That is… I'm a new cat owner, I just moved in with a fiance.
So cute.
Yeah, that is just being an endpoint.
And that is property-level data, floor plan level data, unit level data, and that's delivered on a daily basis.
So, those are kind of the two options that are available.
Api would just be, like, we could decide how much you want to call it, if you only need it once a month, once a week.
But in both those options, if there's… Like, filters, i.
e, you're built on a property, or total unit counts, or property class.
That will help me shape up the actual, like, what actual cost structure would look like.
Got it.

Yeah, for… for my purposes, I would really like the ability to kind of get as wide of a range of data as possible, so kind of wouldn't be… subject to any specific filters, we'd want to kind of cover as much multifamily properties as we could.
Okay.
So we're kind of… Covering the whole, like, U.
S.
and property type.
Okay, and then from a market scope, like, is there select markets you want to do this in, or are you thinking broader….

Again, yeah, I mean… From a cost standpoint, if it makes sense to narrow down by market, we could just, you know, I could give you a list of our, kind of, 10 to 15 target markets, but… Ideally, I would like to cover the whole U.
S.
Yeah, I mean, I know more data is better.
Yeah, exactly.
And it can be cost prohibitive, because it's like, essentially, we've only had full-scope national data sync conversations with, like, very large institutional investors.
And it's, like, you know, in the millions of dollars, and so I'm assuming that's probably not what you all are.
Got it, yeah.
That wouldn't be quite within our range, but yeah.
Right, so, and I can send you a scope, too, of, like, here's just generalized costs, and then if we want to filter it down, we'd be able to do that.
Okay, yeah, that sounds great.
So, the pricing, like, you have, kind of, options by number of queries, or….
Yeah.
Okay.
Like, generally the crisis is very, … The API is just, like, it's how many calls you want, how frequently you want it, and then it's the cost per property that we're pushing over.
The, unit sync, which gets down to, like, individual unit-level, data, that is, generally an additional, like, cost per unit that we put against the subject properties.
So, like, if IPS has… I don't know, 2,500 units or something to that effect.
It's an incremental 20 cents a unit against that, but you're only getting the data that they are currently tracking within their IQ account, if that makes sense.
Got it.
Okay.
So it's much more limited, because it's like the product that is relevant to the subject's properties.
Okay.
But right, you're talking, like, full MSA, you're talking 500,000 units first in that data sink, maybe 10,000 or something to that effect, or 10,000, so… Okay.
So those are the ranges we're dealing with.
All of it is possible, but that's why I wanted to just jump on a call to better understand.
Yeah.
Throwing things across, across the email at you.
Yeah, makes sense.
And so… So you're saying the property level data, that's going to be on a per-property basis, and then zip code and MSA level, it's per query?
Yeah.
Per API call.
Okay.
Exactly, and then we cap the total cost, of each MSA as well, but again, it's like 387 MSAs, so….
in the queue.
It's a thousand in each MSA, so, very high cost if you're getting everything.
Sorry, so you… if we were to pull just, like, historical data on the Austin MSA, like, rolled up.
Average effective rents, asking rents, occupancy.
Yeah.
So, that's another conversation.
If we're going to historical data as well, because effectively, and we have, unit-level data back to October of 2020.

So there's, like, unit growth over that time frame as well.
Some of that is, like, organic, i.
e.
new properties were developed.

the API feed, or the data sync, that is, like, from contract forward, basically.

what you have here is, each MSA… let's see what the…

what's your, I guess, your initial instinct on, like, after seeing… so there's the data sync option, which is… actually, let me share this again.
